ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pasta all'Amatriciana; closest parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for Amatriciana; 'Pasta, cooked' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961009 'Pasta dish' is the closest parent; no specific Amatriciana concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Pasta all'Amatriciana is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 180 g cooked pasta and 120 g sauce with guanciale and Pecorino Romano.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with tomato sauce and meat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Pasta all'Amatriciana as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta (FDC ID 168936), canned tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), cured pork/pancetta (FDC ID 167903), and Pecorino Romano cheese (FDC ID 173420), weighted to a representative recipe ratio (60% pasta, 30% tomato sauce, 8% guanciale/pancetta, 2% cheese per 100 g edible portion). Energy cross-checked against USDA Branded Food entries for similar pasta-with-meat-sauce products. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a restaurant-style Amatriciana.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked, al dente) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pasta all'Amatriciana as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked pasta (spaghetti/bucatini, al dente), which reports GI values in the range 40–50 (mean ~45, reference food glucose = 100). The tomato-based sauce and guanciale fat content are expected to lower the overall GI relative to plain pasta. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 67.5) / 100 = 30.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 22.5 × 3 = 67.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured pork (guanciale/pancetta) and Pecorino Romano cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 1,260 mg sodium — more than half the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates, partially offset by fat and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 150–200 g and choose al dente pasta preparation, as the glycemic load of a full 300 g restaurant serving is approximately 30, which may cause a significant postprandial glucose rise; pairing with a side salad can further blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from cured pork and aged cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Pasta all'Amatriciana occasionally and in reduced portions, as the saturated fat from guanciale and Pecorino Romano (approximately 5.4 g per 300 g serving) and high sodium content are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta all'Amatriciana is a classic Italian pasta dish originating from the town of Amatrice in the Lazio region of central Italy. It is one of the four canonical Roman pasta preparations, alongside Cacio e Pepe, Carbonara, and Gricia. The dish is made by rendering guanciale (cured pork cheek) in a pan until crispy, then deglazing with white wine, adding crushed San Marzano tomatoes, chilli flakes, and black pepper to create a robust, slightly spicy tomato sauce. The sauce is tossed with long pasta — traditionally bucatini or spaghetti — and finished with grated Pecorino Romano cheese. The result is a deeply savoury, umami-rich dish with a balance of acidity from the tomatoes, richness from the pork fat, and sharpness from the aged cheese. Nutritionally, a 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 495 kcal, 20 g protein, 15.6 g fat, and 67.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 1,260 mg per serving) due to the cured pork and aged cheese, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 30, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or dairy allergies. In Singapore, it is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore.
common_user_queries: Is Amatriciana pasta high in sodium?
What is the difference between Amatriciana and Carbonara?
What pasta is used in Amatriciana?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for Pasta all'Amatriciana as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries weighted by a representative recipe ratio. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked pasta (al dente). Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for estimation for the components of this dish, and is considered negligible. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as approximate. The restaurant price of SGD 29.32 from field survey data (Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore, Boat Quay) is preserved from the input record for reference but is not used in nutritional scoring. The dish is not a Singaporean dish; therefore, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi multilingual name fields are not mandatory and are left null. The romanized_local field captures the common short-form name 'Amatriciana' used in Italian and international contexts.
